stem: A biology dataset contains 80 observations of cell size. The data is believed to come from a mixture of two populations: healthy cells (mean ≈ 77.9 µm, SD ≈ 9.5) and abnormal cells (mean ≈ 175.3 µm, SD ≈ 37.2). The proportion of healthy cells in the sample is uncertain, estimated between 40% and 62%. The observed overall mean is 125.6 µm with SD = 55.6. Estimate the true population mean of cell size, accounting for the uncertainty in the mixing proportion.

ground_truth.answer: Mixture mean = w×77.9 + (1-w)×175.3. As w varies from 0.4 to 0.62, the mixture mean ranges from 114.9 to 136.3. With sampling variability, CI = [102.7, 148.5]. Point estimate at w=0.51: 125.6 µm.

solution_steps: Mixture mean formula: μ_mix = w × μ_A + (1-w) × μ_B.
At w=0.4: μ_mix = 136.3 (more of abnormal cells).
At w=0.62: μ_mix = 114.9 (more of healthy cells).
Combined interval [102.7, 148.5] accounts for both mixing uncertainty and sampling variability.
common_failure_modes: Using the simple average of the two component means without weighting
Treating the mixing proportion as known instead of uncertain
Ignoring that the overall SD (55.6) is inflated by the between-component variation
